coredata.dc:title: A magnetically recyclable dual Z-scheme GCNQDs-CoTiO3/CoFe2O4 composite photocatalyst for efficient photocatalytic degradation of oxytetracycline

coredata.dc:description: g-C3N4 exhibits excellent performance in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, but the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs limits its application. Herein, a dual Z-scheme g-C3N4 QDs-CoTiO3/CoFe2O4 (GCC) composite photocatalyst was synthesized by in-situ growth method and ultrasonic impregnation method, which effectively increased the separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and significantly improved the photocatalytic degradation performance. The degradation efficiency of oxytetracycline can reach up to 88% and shows good stability and reusability. At the same time, the GCC composite photocatalyst can be quickly recovered by applying an external magnetic field after the photocatalytic reaction, which reflecting its good magnetic controllability. Furthermore, this work also proposed the possible degradation pathways and degradation mechanisms of the GCC composite photocatalyst to degrade oxytetracycline, which provides efficient way to prepare a low-cost and high-efficiency double Z-scheme heterojunctions.

head.ce:title: A magnetically recyclable dual Z-scheme GCNQDs-CoTiO3/CoFe2O4 composite photocatalyst for efficient photocatalytic degradation of oxytetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: g-C3N4 exhibits excellent performance in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, but the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs limits its application. Herein, a dual Z-scheme g-C3N4 QDs-CoTiO3/CoFe2O4 (GCC) composite photocatalyst was synthesized by in-situ growth method and ultrasonic impregnation method, which effectively increased the separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and significantly improved the photocatalytic degradation performance. The degradation efficiency of oxytetracycline can reach up to 88% and shows good stability and reusability. At the same time, the GCC composite photocatalyst can be quickly recovered by applying an external magnetic field after the photocatalytic reaction, which reflecting its good magnetic controllability. Furthermore, this work also proposed the possible degradation pathways and degradation mechanisms of the GCC composite photocatalyst to degrade oxytetracycline, which provides efficient way to prepare a low-cost and high-efficiency double Z-scheme heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic, belonging to tetracycline antibiotics. OTC is widely used in aquaculture and animal husbandry because of its low cost and therapeutic effect. However, it will have an adverse effect on biology in the case of abuse of antibiotic and improper treatment of pharmaceutical wastewater [1], such as bacterial resistance, antibiotic resistance genes, toxic effects on microbial genes, and even threaten human health [2]. Some countries had set extremely strict standards for residues in order to reduce the threat to human health [3]. Therefore, a green and efficient OTC removal technology has become an urgent need.

Compared with traditional treatment processes, photocatalytic technology has some advantages of high efficiency, environmental protection and low cost, which is considered to be the most promising advanced oxidation process [4–9]. Among the known photocatalysts, graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted wide attention due to its unique band gap structure, excellent thermal stability and chemical properties [10–12]. However, the photocatalytic performance of the original g-C3N4 is unsatisfactory due to the shortcomings of small specific surface area [13] and high electron-hole recombination rate [14]. Therefore, researchers have put a lot of effort into improving the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, such as the morphology engineering of g-C3N4 [15,16], the doping of element [17], the construction of heterojunction [18]. g-C3N4 quantum dots (GCNQDs) are the products of morphology engineering of g-C3N4, which could serve as an electronic acceptor or electronic donor, thereby effectively improving the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and enhancing the photocatalytic performance. For example, Zhou et al. coupled GCNQDs with long-acting phosphors to effectively reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improve the degradation rate of methyl orange [19].

Constructing a heterojunction is also an effective method to improve charge separation of g-C3N4. At present, the semiconductor materials commonly used to construct the heterojunction with g-C3N4 include sulfide [20], phosphide [21], transition metal oxides [22,23], etc. Cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) nanoparticles belong to the mixed valence transition metal oxides with spinel structure (AB2O4, A, B are transition metals, O = oxygen). It has the characteristics of low preparation cost, high stability and good catalytic activity [24]. At the same time, its strong magnetism can efficiently collect the photocatalyst to solve the problem of easy loss of GCNQDs in the separation process, and further realize the efficient separation of the photocatalyst and the solution [24–26].

However, the binary composite photocatalysts are still affected by relatively low charge separation efficiency. Therefore, the ternary composite photocatalyst based on the Z scheme has attracted much attention because of their unique charge migration mechanism, which improves the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and maintains strong redox capabilities into account simultaneously [27]. For example, Jiang et al. prepared the ternary composite WO3/g-C3N4/Bi2O3, which effectively improved the photocatalytic degradation performance, demonstrating the superiority of the dual Z-scheme mechanism [28]. CoTiO3 (CTO) is a metal-based titanate oxide with an ilmenite structure with excellent semiconductor properties [29]. At present, CoTiO3 has been widely used in many fields, such as ceramics, gas sensors, pigments [30]. At the same time, its excellent oxidation performance and photostability have attracted widespread attention in the field of photocatalysis and become a potential candidate material [31].

In this study, a direct dual Z-scheme GCNQDs-CoTiO3/CoFe2O4 (GCC) composite photocatalyst was prepared by a simple in-situ growth method and ultrasonic impregnation method. The photocatalytic performance was evaluated by the degradation of OTC solution. The results showed that GCC composite photocatalysts show excellent photocatalytic activity. In addition, the main active groups and intermediates produced during the photocatalytic degradation of the GCC composite photocatalyst were analyzed, and the possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism was discussed.

GCNQDs was formed based on thermal polymerization, which was similar to previous work [32–34]. In detail, 5.0 g of melamine was put into the crucible of the muffle furnace and raised the temperature to 550 ℃ for 4 h with a ramp rate of 3℃/min. After cooling to room temperature, it was ground into powder to obtain the bulk g-C3N4. 0.1 g bulk g-C3N4 add to 150 mL HNO3 and refluxed at 125℃ for 24 h. After natural cooling, it was washed with ultra-pure water until pH = 7, then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene stainless steel autoclave and keep at 180℃ for 24 h. After the reaction, it was filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain GCNQDs.

CoTiO3 (CTO) was prepared by high temperature calcination method. First, 2.49 g of cobalt acetate was dissolved in 50 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred for 1 h to obtain a clear solution. Then 3.4 g of tetrabutyl titanate was added to the above solution and stirred continuously for 12 h. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and dried overnight at 80 °C. Finally, the precipitates were heated to 700 ℃ at a heating rate of 10℃/min in air atmosphere for 3 h to obtain CoTiO3 nanorods.

GCNQDs/CFO was obtained by in situ growth method. GCNQDs were uniformly dispersed in 40 mL distilled water under ultrasonic treatment. Then, Co (NO3)2·6H2O and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (molar ratio = 1 : 2) was added to the above solution and stirred at room temperature for 2 h to get a clear solution. The pH of the solution was adjusted by adding sodium hydroxide solution (5.0 M) drop by drop until pH = 12, continue stirring for 1 h. The mixture was transferred to a 100 mL high pressure reactor and heat to 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was obtained by centrifugation. The precipitate was cleaned with ethanol and dried overnight at 60 °C. Finally, x GCNQDS/CFO (x = 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8 and 1.0) was obtained, where × is referred to the mass percentage of GCNQDs.

As shown in Scheme 1 , GCC composite photocatalysts are prepared by ultrasonic impregnation method. Under ultrasonic treatment, the optimal ratio of GCNQDs/CFO and y CTO were evenly dispersed in 15 mL of ethanol for 1 h, and then y CTO solution was dropped into GCNQDs/CFO solution for 1 h of ultrasonic treatment. 15 mL of acetone was added to the above solution and stirred for 24 h. The supernatant was poured out after natural precipitation, and dried overnight at 60℃ to obtain y GCC composite photocatalysts (y = 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0), where y refered to the mass percentage of CTO.

The crystal structures and surface electronic states of GCNQDs, CTO, CFO and GCC composite photocatalysts were verified by D/MAX2500 X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument. The morphologies of the samples were verified by SUPERA-55 scanning electron microscopy and JEOL 2100 (JEOL) transmission electron microscopy. The specific surface area, average pore diameter, and pore volume of the sample materials were recorded by ASAP 2460 3.01(Micromeritics) Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET). The optical properties of the samples were measured and recorded by UV1800PC spectrophotometer and Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer. Total organic carbon (TOC) and toxicity test data were collected and recorded on the Multi 2100S TOC/TN instrument and BX-LID-P portable luminous intensity detector. The magnetic property of photocatalysts was studied using Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM) by varying magnetic field from − 15000 to + 15000 Oe.

Oxytetracycline (OTC) solution was used to evaluate the biodegradability of GCC to antibiotics in water. The main method is to weigh 0.03 g composite material and place it in 50 mL OTC solution with a solution concentration of 40 mg/L. After mixing, it is placed in the dark for 2 h to achieve the equilibrium of adsorption–desorption. Then, a photocatalysis device was used to simulate the degradation experiment, and 3 mL solution was absorbed by pipetting gun every 30 min and filtered with a needle filter. The filtered solution was analyzed using UV–Vis spectrophotometer and the maximum absorption wavelength associated with TOC was 363 nm.

The photoelectrochemical characterization experiments were carried out on a standard three-electrode electrochemical analyzer (CHI660D). A 500 W Xe lamp was used as the light source, 0.0025 M K3[Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 M KCl mixed solution as the electrolyte, platinum plate as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and FTO glass (1 × 2 cm2) as the working electrode. The working electrode was prepared by weighing 10 mg of the sample to be tested and 10 mg of iodine and dispersing it in 25 mL of acetone followed by ultrasonic treatment to form a suspension. The FTO glass was then inserted into the solution and electroplating began until the sample area was 1 cm2.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of GCNQDs, CTO, CFO and GCC composite photocatalysts, and the XRD pattern of GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalyst is shown in Fig. S1. It could be found that GCNQDs has two typical diffraction peaks at 27.3° (002) and 13.0° (100), which was attributed to interlayer stacking of the aromatic system and triazine repeat units [33,35,36]. All diffraction peaks of CTO were consistent with the standard card (JCPDS card No. 15–0866). The diffraction peak of CFO was corresponded to the cubic spinel phase, which meet the standard card (JCPDS card No. 22–1086), indicating the formation of single-phase spinel. The prepared GCC composite photocatalyst has typical diffraction peaks of GCNQDs, CTO and CFO, showing the coexistence of three phases. In addition, no other diffraction peaks were detected, indicating the high purity of the GCC composite photocatalysts.

The surface chemical composition and state of GCNQDs, CTO, CFO, 0.8 GCNQDs /CFO and 0.8 GCC were further measured by XPS. As shown in Fig. S2, characteristic peaks of C, N, O, Ti, Co and Fe can be observed in the XPS survey spectrum, indicating the successful preparation of the composite photocatalysts. Fig. 2 is high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, Ti 2p, Co 2p and Fe 2p in the 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts. The comparison of high-resolution XPS spectra for the elements in the remaining samples is shown in Fig. S3. The characteristic peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.12 eV and 288.94 eV in the C 1 s spectrum of 0.8 GCC (Fig. 2a) correspond to the reference C–C, C-O and C = O, respectively [15,37]. In Fig. 2b, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum can be divided into three characteristic peaks located at 398.28 eV, 398.88 eV and 400.8 eV, corresponding to N = N-C, N-(C)3 and C-NH2, respectively [38]. Interestingly, compared with 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalysts (Fig. S3a and b), the binding energy of C 1 s and N 1 s in 0.8 GCC moved to a higher binding energy after adding CTO, indicating the presence of charge transfer and tight interaction between GCNQDs/CFO and CTO. Fig. 2c shows the O 1 s XPS spectrum, which has been fitted with two characteristic peaks. The characteristic peak at 529.94 eV was typical M−O (M = Co, Fe, Ti), and the peak at 531.34 eV could be attributed to O–H (surface OH–, adsorbed O2 –) [39,40]. In Fig. 2d, Ti 2p can be fitted into three characteristic peaks. The characteristic peaks at 458.01 eV and 463.78 eV are attributed to Ti4+ 2p3/2 and Ti4+ 2p1/2 [31,41], and the characteristic peak at 459.02 eV may be Ti2+ 2p1/2, which is consistent with the results of previous studies [42,43]. In the Co 2p spectra (Fig. 2e), the two spin–orbit doublet characteristic peaks of Co 2p3/2 are located at 780.04 eV and 781.42 eV. The characteristic peak at 796.34 eV corresponds to Co 2p1/2, indicating the presence of Co2+ and Co3+. The peaks at 784.62 eV and 802.64 eV are the vibration satellite peaks [39]. The Fe 2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 2f. The peaks at 717.94 eV and 732.54 eV are vibration satellite peaks, and the characteristic peak located at 724.24 eV corresponds to Fe 2p1/2. The peaks at 710.54 eV and 713.58 eV are composed of two spin–orbit doublets characteristics of Fe 2p3/2 [31,44]. Compared with the 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalysts, the peak positions of O 1 s and Co 2p in the 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts were negatively shifted, suggesting that CTO obtained the electrons from GCNQDs/CFO, which is also consisted with above the result of C 1 s and N 1 s XPS spectrum. These results further demonstrated the successful preparation of GCC composite photocatalysts and their strong interfacial interaction [45,46].

The typical morphologies of CTO, CFO and GCC composite photocatalysts were observed by the s97canning electron microscope (SEM). As shown in Fig. 3 a, CTO presents a hexagonal rod structure. From the magnified SEM image (Fig. 3d), it can be seen that the CTO presents a hollow rod-like structure consisted of a large number of nanoparticles. CFO is a nanocrystal structure with regular particles (Fig. 3b, e). For 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst (Fig. 3c, f), the rod-like structure of CTO was still maintained but the surface of CTO became rough. Meanwhile, the element map (Fig. 3g) can verify that C, N, O, Fe and Co are uniformly distributed on the CTO surface, which indicated the successful loading of CFO and GCNQDs.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) was further used to study the crystal structure in detail. It can be seen from Fig. 4 a that the CFO presents uniform size with average diameter ca. 15 nm. In the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) diagram, the lattice fringes can be observed clearly, and the lattice spacing of about 0.253 nm can be assigned to the (311) crystal plane of CFO [47]. Fig. 4b-c shows the TEM images of the rod-shaped CTO. According to the magnified HRTEM image and profile lines in Fig. 4c, the lattice spacing is determined to 0.371 nm, corresponding to the (012) crystal plane of CTO [48]. As shown in Fig. 4d-e, GCNQDs possess circles with relatively uniform particle size, with an average particle size of about 2.5 nm and the corresponding lattice spacing is calculated to 0.247 nm according to the magnified HRTEM image and profile lines in Fig. 4d. The profile lines in TEM images of GCC show the crystal faces of GCNQDs (100), CTO (012) and CFO (012), confirming the coexistence of the three phases of GCNQDs, CTO and CFO (Fig. 4f-h). In addition, energy spectrum point scanning (EDX) was performed on GCC composite photocatalyst to verify the presence of C, N, O, Ti, Co and Fe elements (Fig. S4).

The photocatalytic activity of GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalysts was related to its ability to degrade 40 mg/L OTC solution (pH = 7) under UV-light. The blank experiment (Fig. S5) verified that OTC reaction solution would not decompose without photocatalysts, indicating that the photocatalytic degradation of OTC itself could be ignored. Fig. 5 a shows the photodegradation effect of OTC under irradiation. It could be seen that the degradation efficiency of GCNQDs with regulated morphology was better than that of the bulk g-C3N4. In addition, the degradation ability of 0.1–1.0 GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalysts (degradation efficiency: 49%-65%) were stronger than that of the pure sample GCNQDs (degradation efficiency: 30%). Among these GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalysts, 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalysts showed improved degradation efficiency, but only about 49%, which may be attributed to the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in such binary composite photocatalysts. In Fig. 5b, the photodegradation efficiency conforms to the first-order kinetic equation
l n

= k t
, where t and k represent the time constant and rate constant respectively. The degradation rate of 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO was 0.00455 min−1, about 1.8 times that of GCNQDs (0.00250 min−1). Therefore, 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO was selected to prepare a series of ternary composite photocatalysts.

Based on the best load ratio of GCNQDs, GCC ternary composite photocatalysts were prepared. As shown in Fig. 6 a, 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts exhibit the best photocatalytic degradation ability, and the degradation efficiency can reach up to 88%. The addition of CTO improved the transfer rate of photogenerated electrons and inhibited the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, so as to improve the photocatalytic degradation ability. However, the addition of excessive CTO would increase the turbidity, which further affected the light transmittance, resulting in the reduction of photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The photocatalytic properties of other photocatalysts for OTC degradation are listed in Table S1 and show that 0.8 GCC can degrade high concentration OTC solution with relatively small amount of catalyst and relatively short degradation time. In Fig. 6c, the photocatalytic degradation rate of GCC composite photocatalysts conforms to the first-order kinetic equation. When the dosage of CTO was 0.8, it showed the highest degradation activity, and the degradation rate was about 0.01409 min−1. It was 3.09 and 4.79 times higher than 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO (0.00455 min−1) and CTO (0.00294 min−1) respectively. Therefore, regulating the dosage of CTO in the preparation of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts is an important link to affect the photocatalytic degradation efficiency.

In order to further understand the influence of pH conditions on OTC degradation of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts during photocatalytic degradation, the pH value of the solution was adjusted from 3 to 11. As shown in Fig. 6b, when pH values were controlled at 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11, the OTC removal rates are 43%, 48%, 88%, 82%, and 73%. The degradation rate constant (Fig. 6d) is about 0.00385 min−1. 0.00434 min−1, 0.01409 min−1, 0.01169 min−1 and 0.00892 min−1. The results showed that OTC had the highest removal efficiency and showed the fastest degradation rate when pH = 7; the removal efficiency and degradation rate of OTC decreased slightly when pH > 7 and the removal efficiency and degradation efficiency of OTC decreased significantly when pH < 7. Therefore, it is believed that OTC is easier to remove under neutral or alkaline conditions, which is consistent with the results of previous studies [9,49]. It can be seen from Fig. S6 that the 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts exhibit a positive charge under acidic conditions, and a negative charge under alkaline conditions. In addition, OTC is an amphoteric substance (pKa = 3.57, 7.49 and 9.88) [50] and its existing form depends on the pH of the water. Namely, OTC mainly exists in the form of positive charge (OTC+), molecular form and negative charge (OTC-, OTC2-) under acidic, neutral and alkaline conditions, respectively [49,51]. Therefore, the reasons for the decrease of OTC removal rate under acidic or alkaline conditions are speculated. On the one hand, there is electrostatic repulsion between 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts and OTC which hinders the interaction. On the other hand, the active substances of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts are affected. In the acidic system, ·O2 reacts with h+ to generate H2O2 [9]. In the alkaline condition, ·O2 decreases gradually with the increase of pH value [49]. It is further speculated that ·O2 and h+ may be the main active groups for OTC degradation in 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts, which has been confirmed in the following active group capture experiment.

The effect of initial concentration on photocatalytic degradation OTC solution for 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts is shown in Fig. 7 a. When the initial concentration is 10–30 mg/L, the degradation rate of OTC can reach 95%. With the increase of the initial concentration, the degradation rate of OTC gradually decreased. Under the same dosage of the 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts, the active sites are much lower than the active sites required for OTC degradation [52]. Moreover, the higher initial concentration means that a higher concentration of intermediates will be produced, thus it would compete with OTC for the active group [51]. In addition, the influence of inorganic ions (HPO4 2-, HCO3 –, Cl-and SO4 2-) that may exist in the actual water environment on the photocatalytic degradation efficiency was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 7b, the effects of various ions on the photocatalytic activity from strong to weak are: HPO4 2->SO4 2->Cl->HCO3 –. There may be two reasons for this inhibition phenomenon: (1) Inorganic ions and OTC molecules occupied the active sites on the surface of the 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts, and they were competitive adsorption [53]; (2) Inorganic ions react with the active group or become scavengers to inhibit the activity of the active components [54].

In order to further analyze the main active components in the photocatalytic degradation of OTC by 0.8 GCC, isopropanol (IPA), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and benzoquinone (BQ) were added to the reaction process as active species capture agents of hydroxyl radical (·OH), hole radical (h+) and superoxide radical (·O2 –), respectively. Fig. 7c clearly shows that the photocatalytic activity is significantly inhibited after the addition of BQ and IPA. It demonstrated that that hydroxyl radical (·OH) and superoxide radical (·O2 –) were the main active components for the photocatalytic degradation of OTC over 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst. However, the catalytic activity of hole radical (h+) was a little change after the addition of EDTA, indicating that hole radical (h+) was not the main active group. In order to further verify the role of different active groups, cryogenic in situ electron spin resonance (ESR) technique was used. In dark conditions, no characteristic peaks of free radicals can be found. After adding the mercury lamp, 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst showed obvious ·O2-characteristic peaks (Fig. 7d). In addition, the four peaks of ·OH also increased significantly, with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 (Fig. 7e) [55]. These results proved that ·O2– and ·OH were the main reactive groups in the reaction system, which were consistent with the results of the trapping experiment.

Four cycles of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts were carried out in order to further study the stability of the composite (Fig. 7f). For each degradation experiment, 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts were collected by centrifugation. After centrifugation, the composite photocatalysts were washed with ethanol and distilled water and dried at 60 ℃ overnight. Finally, they were transferred to the freshly prepared OTC solution for degradation experiment. As shown in Fig. 7f, the removal rate of OTC can still remain above 85% after four cycles, indicating that 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts have good stability. The slight decrease in degradation efficiency may be due to the slight agglomeration of GCNQDs caused by drying during the collection of composite photocatalysts. Moreover, the degraded products may be attached to the surface of composite photocatalysts, reducing part of the active sites. At the same time, XRD, SEM and TEM characterization experiments were carried out on the composite photocatalysts before and after degradation. As can be seen from Fig. 8 a, the results of the composite photocatalyst before and after degradation are consistent, and no impurity peaks appeared. In Fig. S7, composite photocatalysts still retain the original shape. These results fully indicate that 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts has good stability.

The specific surface area was one of the factors that affect the photocatalytic degradation. Therefore, brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) process was used to study the specific surface area of GCNQDs, CFO, 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO, CTO and 0.8 GCC composite. It can be seen that N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms are typical type IV curves with H3-type hysteresis loops (Fig. 8b). Table 1 shows the specific surface area, pore size and pore volume of the measured samples. The specific surface area of GCNQDs is about 25.0292 cm2/g. After being combined with CFO (7.1169 cm2/g), the specific surface area of 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalysts (1.7763 cm2/g) is significantly reduced. It may be that when the composite photocatalysts were prepared by the in-situ growth method, the pores collapsed and formed into a single particle which occupied the surface of the composite photocatalysts. After adding CTO (25.3362 cm2/g), the specific surface area of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst (46.0426 cm2/g) increased significantly which was about 25.92 times of that of 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO composite photocatalysts. These properties are consistent with the removal efficiency of OTC, indicating that the addition of CTO helps to expose the active site and improve the degradation performance of the composite photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transformation mechanism of oxytetracycline and toxicity evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the OTC photocatalytic degradation path of 0.8 GCC composites, various intermediates and final products were identified by LC-MS analysis. The matched molecular weight is shown in Fig. S8, and possible intermediates are listed in Table S2. The relative molecular weight error of all intermediates is within ± 2. In the present study, 15 intermediates were analyzed and four possible photocatalytic degradation pathways were proposed (Fig. 9 ). According to previous reports, OTC degradation processes include demethylation, decarbonization, decarbonylation, deoxidation and ring-opening [9,51,56]. Pathway Ⅰ was dominated by decarbonylation with α-cleavage [51]. OTC (m/z = 461) generated mono-hydroxylated products (m/z = 483) after α-cleavage, followed by a part of ring closure reaction. Pathway Ⅱ generated intermediates at m/z = 274 by directly breaking C–C bond, and then separated ring into another intermediate. Pathway III mainly involved OTC conversion and decomposition through a series of demethylation and hydroxyl groups [57], followed by deamination and dihydroxylation to produce m/z = 274, which would be cracked and oxidized to produce ring opening products such as m/z = 116. Pathway Ⅳ may separate the ring from the OTC molecule by demethylation and simultaneous cleavage of the C–C bond to form intermediates at m/z = 303 and 146 [51]. Decarbonylation was then performed, resulting in a gradual reduction in concentration and the emergence of new products (m/z = 116, 258 and 276) [58]. With the passage of time, the concentration of new products (m/z = 116, 258 and 276) gradually increased. All of these intermediates could be further mineralized into CO2, H2O and some inorganic ions. The total organic carbon concentration (TOC) of the degraded OTC solution was further studied. As shown in Fig. 8c, TOC residue of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts was the lowest (1524 μg/L) among all tested samples and the mineralization rate was 83.0%, but there was still a certain amount of organic acid residue. This may be due to incomplete mineralization of some specific functional groups. At the same time, its toxicity was studied. In Fig. 8d, 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts had the lowest inhibition rate on luminescent bacteria, indicating that the degraded solution had low toxicity.

A vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) with a 15,000 Oe magnetic field was used for studying the magnetic properties of samples at room temperature. As shown in the Fig. 10 a, the 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst exhibits ferromagnetic behavior, reflecting its good magnetic controllability. CFO saturation magnetization (Ms) was 51.6 emu/g, coercivity (Hc) was 492.8 Oe, and residual magnetization (Mr) was 14.1 emu/g. After adding GCNQDs, the saturation magnetization (7.5 emu/g) decreased significantly, and after combining with CTO, the saturation magnetization increased slightly, about 26.3 emu/g, while the coercivity and residual magnetization were 1417.9 Oe and 11.3 emu/g, respectively. This may be that the increase of Co content leads to a slight increase in saturation magnetization [59,60]. As can be seen in the illustration, 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts can be easily separated from the solution by magnets.

The optical and electrochemical characterization including PL spectrum, photoelectrochemcial (PEC) measurement, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test, UV–vis diffuse reflectance (DRS) and Mott-Schottky (MS) curve of the samples were performed. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was used to analyze the photoinduced carrier recombination rate of the prepared samples. In general, the higher the PL intensity, the worse the separation performance of photogenerated carriers, resulting in the lower photocatalytic activity of semiconductors [61]. Fig. 10b shows the PL spectra of the samples excited at 350 nm. It can be found that the addition of CTO greatly reduces the photogenerated carrier recombination rate of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst, and the luminescence peak intensity at 450 nm constantly changes with the dosage of CTO. When the mass fraction of CTO was 0.8, the emission peak intensity reached the lowest, indicating that 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst had higher separation capacity and transfer efficiency.

The photoelectrochemcial (PEC) measurement and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test of as-prepared samples were performed on a standard three-electrode unit. As shown in Fig. 10c, the photocurrent of 0.8 GCC (0.33 μA/cm2) composite photocatalysts is much stronger than those of GCNQDs (0.07 μA/cm2), CTO (0.16 μA/cm2), CFO (0.12 μA/cm2) and 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO (0.22 μA/cm2), which demonstrated that the composite photocatalyst inhibited the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increased the separation and transfer rate of photogenerated carriers. In the EIS Nyquist diagram (Fig. 10d), the relative arc sizes of the prepared samples decreased successively GCNQDs > CFO > CTO > 0.8 GCNQDs/CFO > 0.8 GCC. It was indicated that 0.8 GCC composite photocatalysts had the lowest charge transfer resistance and the highest interfacial charge transfer rate. These results proved that the combination of GCNQDs with CTO and CFO could effectively improve the charge separation performance of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst.

To evaluate the light-response range and energy-band structure of composite photocatalysts on their photocatalytic properties, the prepared samples were tested by UV–vis diffuse reflectance (DRS). As shown in Fig. 11 a, GCNQDs have a typical absorption edge around 450 nm, and CFO shows a straight line possibly because the samples are black. Interestingly, two distinct absorption edges could be seen for CoTiO3, with strong absorption bands extending from the ultraviolet to the visible region and even into the near-infrared region. This could be explained by the splitting of the CoTiO3 crystal. The sharp edge at 500 nm is due to the O2–→Ti4+ charge transfer interaction, while the absorption edge at 690 nm is due to the Co2+→Ti4+ charge transfer band. 0.8 GCC showed a red shift compared with pure CoTiO3. The Kubelka-Mink function was usually used to estimate the band gap energy:
α
h v
= A
h v -
E g
1 2
, where α, h, v, Eg and A are the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, optical frequency, band energy and constant, respectively. The corresponding band gap is calculated according to this calculation method. As shown in Fig. 11b, the band gap of GCNQDs is about 2.97 eV, while that of CTO and CFO is about 2.46 eV and 1.88 eV, respectively. Compared with the band gap of the bulk GCN (2.78 eV), that of GCNQDs increased slightly. This could be caused by quantum confinement effects [62,63]. As the semiconductor particle size gradually decreases, the band gap value will increase correspondingly, so the band gap of GCNQDs will be slightly wider than the bulk GCN. In addition, the charge transport mechanism of 0.8 GCC composite photocatalyst was studied according to the M−S curve with different frequency (Fig. 11c-e). The tangents were drawn for the line segments of the M−S curves of GCNQDs, CTO and CFO. It could be found that the slope of tangent lines of GCNQDs, CTO and CFO were all positive, indicating that they were n-type semiconductors. By extending the tangent line to the intersection of the X-axis, the flat band potential of GCNQDs, CTO and CFO can be estimated as −1.33 eV, −0.18 eV and 0.32 eV, respectively [64]. For n-type semiconductors, generally the CB position (ECB) has a more negative value relative to Ag/AgCl than the normal H2 electrode (NHE), about 0.2 eV [20]. Therefore, according to the formula
E
NHE
=
E
Ag / A g C l
+ 0.197 e V
[65], the ECB values of GCNQDs, CTO and CFO were calculated to be −1.13 V, 0.02 V and 0.52 V, respectively. The VB position (EVB) values of them were calculated to be 1.84 V, 2.48 V and 2.40 V based on the band gap value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed photocatalytic mechanism of GCC heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above discussion and experimental results, the possible photocatalytic mechanism of the sample is proposed as shown in Fig. 12 . Fig. 12a shows the traditional electron transfer pattern. Under ultraviolet light, e- of GCNQDs CB was transferred to CB of CTO and CFO, while h+ of CTO and CFO was transferred to VB of GCNQDs, thus achieving the separation of e- and h+. At the same time, it could be seen that the CB and VB positions of CTO and CFO were lower than the standard redox potential of O2/·O2 – and H2O/·OH– [29], so O2 and H2O could not be converted into ·O2– and ·OH–, which was contrary to the results of the trapping experiments. Therefore, the separation and transfer process of photogenerated electron-hole pairs should not follow the common heterojunction process.

A more reasonable dual Z-scheme was proposed as shown in Fig. 12b. GCNQDs, CTO and CFO changed their photogenerated electron-hole pairs under UV irradiation. First e- in CB of CFO would be transferred and recombined with h+ in VB of GCNQDs. Subsequently, e- in CB of CTO also combined with h+ in VB of GCNQDs, and the carrier migration resulted in the aggregation of e- in CB of GCNQDs, while h+ was retained in CTO and CFO. Therefore, e- retained in CB of GCNQDs could be captured by O2 to further generate ·O2 –. Meanwhile, h+ in VB of CTO and CFO would oxidize H2O to form ·OH–, and subsequently generate ·O2 – and ·OH– to participate in OTC removal. It could be seen that the photocatalytic reaction of GCC composite photocatalysts follows the dual Z-scheme mechanism, which accelerates the rapid separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs while maintaining strong redox capacity to achieve effective OTC degradation (Scheme 2 ). The reaction process could be described as follows:
GCC + hv → CTO (e-+h+) /GCNQDs (e-+h+) /CFO (e-+h+)(1)
CTO (e-) + GCNQDs (h+) → GCNQDs (recombination)(2)
CFO (e-) + GCNQDs (h+) → GCNQDs (recombination)(3)
GCNQDs (e-) + O2 → ·O2 –(4)
CTO (h+) + H2O→ ·OH–(5)
CFO (h+) + H2O→ ·OH–(6)
·O2 – + ·OH– + h+ + OTC → degradation products(7)

In summary, a dual Z-scheme GCC composite photocatalyst was synthesized by in-situ growth method and ultrasonic impregnation method. The dual Z-scheme heterojunction was formed by the appropriate energy band structure between CTO, CFO and GCNQDs, which greatly reduced the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and effectively improved the degradation efficiency of OTC organic pollutants. At the same time, the possible degradation pathway of OTC was analyzed and studied in detail. In the cycle test, GCC composite photocatalysts have good stability, and due to the ferromagnetic characteristics of CFO, GCC composite photocatalysts can be separated to achieve efficient recycling under external magnetic field conditions. Therefore, this work is helpful to enrich the ideas of the preparation method of double Z-scheme heterojunction, and is conducive to the further development of environmental wastewater purification research.
